dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at 1234 Elm Street.
Hi, my name's Brenda. I'm an EMT here. What made you call 911 today?
I can't breathe. It's been getting worse for days.
Can you tell me your full name and the day of the week?
I'm Patricia. And it's Monday, September 19th.
You're speaking clearly and your airway is open. Have you ever had trouble clearing your airway?
I'm hearing expiratory wheezes when listening. Are they constant or intermittent?
It's been going on for days but is worse this morning.
Pulse is strong and regular, and skin looks warm and pink.
Your breathing is about 18 times a minute-do you tolerate that well?
Pulse is 95 beats per minute, regular rhythm.
Breathing is 18 per minute and does not look labored.
Blood glucose isn't measurable - we'll note it as 0.
Blood pressure reading is 91 over 55.
SpO2 is 99%, and you're on room air.
RFD already applied a non-rebreather mask at 10 LPM. We'll keep it on.
Let's start a 4-lead EKG to monitor continuously.
I'm also noted to be administering albuterol and ipratropium via the nebulizer at 6 LPM. You're not currently in pain.
Breathing is down to about 16 per minute. That's an improvement.
Capnography has been applied and shows 38 mmHg. Let's note that.
You're fully alert and oriented. GCS is 15, which is normal and expected for someone this aware.
When exactly did your breathing issues start, Patricia?
It began on Monday. It's come and gone, but it's really bad now.
Have you noticed the color or texture of any sputum you're coughing?
It looks yellow and greenish. Not a good sign.
Have you had episodes like this in the past?
Yes. I have COPD and Primary Hypertension, and I do get asthma flares as well.
You mentioned hypertension-do you take medications for that condition?
Yes. I take Amlodipine and Losartan for blood pressure.
This morning, when you woke up, was it hard for you to move?
I couldn't get out of bed - too short of breath.
Did you speak with your doctor before calling 911?
Yes. My doctor told me to call an ambulance.
Any known allergies? For example, to antibiotics or pain meds?
I'm allergic to penicillin and ibuprofen.
What medications are you currently using regularly?
I take albuterol, Caduet, Advair, Microzide, and a few others.
That's good coverage for several conditions. Any others, such as statins or aspirin?
Yes. I take aspirin, atorvastatin, fluticasone, and others, but not all daily.
Right. Caduet includes both Amlodipine and Atorvastatin, right? Other meds include?
I also take Golytely, lacosamide, lactulose, Melatonin, and quetiapine, but those aren't used daily.
Are you experiencing any additional pain or discomfort besides the breathing issue?
No pain associated with the breathing issue.
I'm about to administer albuterol and ipratropium via the nebulizer at 6 LPM.
Pulse decreased to 82. Breathing is 16 per minute.
Blood pressure is now 103 over 56. SpO2 is 98% on the non-rebreather.
The nebulizer started to take effect - wheezing is softer now.
EKG still shows normal sinus rhythm-no new abnormalities detected.
We'll continue the respiratory distress protocol. We'll monitor vitals and EKG closely en route.
We'll take you to VCU for care. Is that okay?
Yes, I'm fine with that.
We'll assist you to the cot now. Please stay with us and follow my instructions.
We've got you on the cot now. Your oxygen mask is secure, EKG leads on.
Inside the ambulance, we'll keep you comfortable and stable. We will monitor continuously.
Pulse and breathing are still steady at 82 and 16, respectively.
I'll adjust the EKG leads and recheck capnography to ensure all records are up.
I've called ahead and updated the hospital with your condition and medications given.
Patient is secure, and we are traveling to VCU without lights or sirens.
I'm contacting the hospital for final verification of care details and medication logs.
We're at the hospital now. I'll speak with the ER team to pass along all the details.
Patient is now moving from the ambulance cot to the hospital bed, assisted.
Consent was signed prior to arrival and the patient is ready for ER treatment.
We've fully transitioned care to the hospital staff and provided all patient records.
The patient is stable, and we evacuated the ambulance to VCU.
